unit.name: Receptive-field analysis, temporal shift invariance, and 3D filter visualization
unit.description: Compares late fusion, early fusion, and 3D CNNs through toy receptive-field examples and explains temporal shift invariance.
unit.summary: The instructor uses toy architectures to compare how temporal receptive fields develop in late fusion, early fusion, and 3D CNNs [ts=1306s] [ts=1333s]. In late fusion, 2D layers keep the temporal dimension unchanged while building spatial receptive fields, then a global average pool collapses all time at the end [ts=1340s] [ts=1403s] [ts=1422s]. In early fusion, time is collapsed at the very beginning by treating 3 x 20 channels as one enlarged channel axis, then the network builds spatial receptive fields like a 2D CNN [ts=1440s] [ts=1466s]. In contrast, a 3D CNN builds both spatial and temporal receptive fields gradually through 3D convolutions and 3D pooling, which is why it is described as slow fusion [ts=1498s] [ts=1561s]. A deeper comparison explains that early-fusion 2D filters extend fully over time, so they lack temporal shift invariance: the same transition occurring at different time steps would need separate filters [ts=1605s] [ts=1635s] [ts=1684s]. 3D filters span only a local temporal window and slide over time, so they can reuse filters to recognize the same motion pattern at any moment, and learned 3D kernels can be visualized as video clips showing colors, edges, and motion directions [ts=1739s] [ts=1789s] [ts=1813s] [ts=1883s].

key_points.text: Late fusion builds spatial receptive field gradually but builds temporal receptive field all at once at the final global pooling layer.
Early fusion builds temporal receptive field all at once in the first layer, then builds space gradually.
3D CNNs build receptive fields gradually in both space and time, which motivates the term slow fusion.
Early-fusion 2D convolution is local in space but extends fully in time, causing no temporal shift invariance.
3D convolution uses a local temporal window that slides over time, enabling temporal shift invariance and filter reuse.
Learned 3D filters can be visualized as video clips, revealing color, edge, and motion-sensitive kernels.

kp_catalog.name: Early fusion
kp_catalog.description: Early fusion stacks frames along channels so the first 2D convolution compares frames and collapses time.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because early fusion clarifies early fusion stacks frames along channels so the first 2D convolution compares frames and collapses time, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: 3D CNN and slow fusion
kp_catalog.description: 3D CNNs use 3D convolution and pooling to gradually fuse spatial and temporal information across layers.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because 3D CNN and slow fusion formalizes how 3D CNNs use 3D convolution and pooling to gradually fuse spatial and temporal information across layers, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Temporal receptive field and shift invariance
kp_catalog.description: 3D convolution uses local temporal filters that slide over time, enabling temporal shift invariance unlike early fusion.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because temporal receptive field and shift invariance formalizes how 3D convolution uses local temporal filters that slide over time, enabling temporal shift invariance unlike early fusion, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.
